content:
# Exodus Chapter 31 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~51v–52r (PDF pp. 102–103); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 74–75 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_031_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_031_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_031_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_031_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Calling of Bezalel and Oholiab (vv. 1–11)

**1** And YHWH spoke [**vayedabber YHWH**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**], saying [**le'mor**]:

**2** "See [**re'eh**], I have called [**qarati**] by name [**veshem**] Bezalel [**Betsal'el**] son of [**ben**] Uri [**Uri**], son of [**ben**] Hur [**Chur**], of the tribe of [**lematteh**] Judah [**Yehudah**].

**3** And I have filled [**va'amalle**] him [**oto**] with the Spirit of [**ruach**] God [**Elohim**], in wisdom [**bechokhmah**] and in understanding [**uvitevunah**] and in knowledge [**uveda'at**] and in all [**uvekhol**] workmanship [**melakhah**],

**4** to devise [**lachshov**] designs [**machashavot**], to work [**la'asot**] in gold [**bazzahav**] and in silver [**uvakkesef**] and in bronze [**uvanechoshet**],

**5** and in cutting [**uvacharoshet**] of stone [**even**] for setting [**lemallot**], and in carving [**uvacharoshet**] of wood [**ets**], to work [**la'asot**] in all [**bekhol**] workmanship [**melakhah**].

**6** And I [**va'ani**], behold [**hinneh**], I have given [**natatti**] with him [**itto**] Oholiab [**et-Oholiav**] son of [**ben**] Ahisamach [**Achisamakh**], of the tribe of [**lematteh**] Dan [**Dan**]; and in the heart of [**uvelev**] every [**kol**] wise-hearted one [**chakham-lev**] I have put [**natatti**] wisdom [**chokhmah**], and they shall make [**ve'asu**] all [**et kol**] that [**asher**] I have commanded you [**tsivvitikha**]:

**7** the Tent of [**et ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**], and the Ark [**ve'et-ha'aron**] for the Testimony [**la'edut**], and the mercy seat [**ve'et-hakkaporet**] that [**asher**] is upon it [**alav**], and all [**ve'et kol**] the vessels of [**keley**] the Tent [**ha'ohel**],

**8** and the table [**ve'et-hashulchan**] and its vessels [**ve'et-kelav**], and the pure [**hattahor**] lampstand [**hammenorah**] and all [**ve'et-kol**] its vessels [**keleyha**], and the altar of [**ve'et mizbeyach**] incense [**haqqetoret**],

**9** and the altar of [**ve'et mizbeyach**] burnt offering [**ha'olah**] and all [**ve'et-kol**] its vessels [**kelav**], and the basin [**ve'et-hakkiyyor**] and its stand [**ve'et-kanno**],

**10** and the woven [**ve'et bigdey**] garments [**hasserad**], and the holy [**et-bigdey**] garments [**haqqodesh**] for Aaron [**le'Aharon**] the priest [**hakkohen**], and the garments of [**ve'et-bigdey**] his sons [**vanav**], to minister as priests [**lekhahhen**],

**11** and the oil of [**ve'et shemen**] anointing [**hammishchah**], and the incense of [**ve'et-qetoret**] fragrant spices [**hassammim**] for the Holy Place [**laqqodesh**]; according to all [**kekhol**] that [**asher**] I have commanded you [**tsivvitikha**] they shall do [**ya'asu**]."

---

## The Sabbath as a Sign (vv. 12–17)

**12** And YHWH spoke [**vayedabber YHWH**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**], saying [**le'mor**]:

**13** "And you [**ve'attah**], speak [**dabber**] to [**el**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**], saying [**le'mor**]: 'Surely [**akh**] My Sabbaths [**et-shabbtotay**] you shall keep [**tishmoru**], for [**ki**] it [**hi**] is a sign [**ot**] between Me [**beyni**] and between you [**uveyneykhem**] throughout your generations [**ledorotheyekhem**], to know [**lada'at**] that [**ki**] I [**ani**] am YHWH [**YHWH**] who sanctifies you [**meqaddishkhem**].

**14** And you shall keep [**ushmartem**] the Sabbath [**et-hashabbat**], for [**ki**] it [**hi**] is holy [**qodesh**] to you [**lakhem**]; the one who profanes it [**mechalelayha**] shall surely be put to death [**mot yumat**], for [**ki**] everyone [**kol**] who does [**ha'oseh**] work [**melakhah**] on it [**bah**] — that soul [**hanefesh hahi**] shall be cut off [**venikhretah**] from the midst of [**miqqerev**] her people [**ammeyha**].

**15** Six [**sheshet**] days [**yamim**] work [**melakhah**] shall be done [**ye'aseh**], and on the seventh [**uvayom hashevi'i**] day [**shabbat**] is a Sabbath [**shabbaton**] of complete rest [**qodesh**], holy [**laYHWH**] to YHWH; everyone [**kol**] who does [**ha'oseh**] work [**melakhah**] on the Sabbath [**beyom hashabbat**] day [**mot**] shall surely be put to death [**yumat**].

**16** And the Sons of Israel [**vesham'ru veney Yisra'el**] shall keep [**et-hashabbat**] the Sabbath [**la'asot**], to observe [**et-hashabbat**] the Sabbath [**ledorotam**] throughout their generations [**berit**] as a covenant [**olam**] forever.

**17** Between Me [**beyni**] and between [**uveyn**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**] it [**hi**] is a sign [**ot**] forever [**le'olam**], for [**ki**] in six [**sheshet**] days [**yamim**] YHWH made [**asah YHWH**] the heavens [**et-hashamayim**] and the earth [**ve'et-ha'arets**], and on the seventh [**uvayom hashevi'i**] day [**shavat**] He ceased [**vayyinnafash**] and was refreshed.' "

---

## The Two Tablets of the Testimony (v. 18)

**18** And He gave [**vayyitten**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**], when He finished [**kekhaloto**] speaking [**ledabber**] with him [**itto**] on Mount [**behar**] Sinai [**Sinay**], the two [**shney**] tablets of [**luchot**] the Testimony [**ha'edut**], tablets of [**luchot**] stone [**even**], written [**ketuvim**] by the finger of [**be'etsba**] God [**Elohim**].

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Terms

**Betsal'el ben Uri ben Chur** (v. 2) -- "Bezalel son of Uri son of Hur." The name Betsal'el (from b-ts-l, "shadow" + 'el, "God") means "in the shadow of God" or "under the protection of God." This theophoric name is profoundly fitting for the chief artisan of the Tabernacle, the structure that will house the divine Presence and cast, as it were, the shadow of God over Israel's encampment. The genealogy traces Bezalel to Hur (Chur), who appears alongside Aaron as Moses's supporter during the battle with Amalek (Exod 17:10-12) and who, according to later tradition (Josephus, Ant. 3.54; cf. 1 Chr 2:19-20), was the son of Caleb and Miriam. If this genealogical connection is correct, Bezalel descends from Moses's own sister, making the Tabernacle a family enterprise at the highest level. The tribe of Judah, from which Bezalel comes, will later provide the royal line of David -- the connection between the artisan of YHWH's dwelling place and the tribe that produces YHWH's anointed king is no coincidence in the canonical structure. The LXX renders the name as Beseleel huion Ouriou tou Ōr ("Beseleël son of Ourios of Ōr"), preserving the Hebrew genealogy in Greek transliteration.

**ruach Elohim** (v. 3) -- "Spirit of God." The phrase ruach Elohim appears first in Genesis 1:2, where the Spirit of God hovers over the face of the deep at creation. Its reappearance here at the construction of the Tabernacle establishes a deliberate parallel: just as the Spirit of God was the operative agent in the creation of the cosmos, so the Spirit of God is the operative agent in the construction of the micro-cosmos, YHWH's dwelling place. The four terms that specify the Spirit's endowment -- chokhmah ("wisdom"), tevunah ("understanding"), da'at ("knowledge"), and melakhah ("workmanship, skill") -- echo the wisdom vocabulary of Proverbs 8, where personified Wisdom is present with God at creation (Prov 8:22-31). Bezalel is thus portrayed as a Spirit-filled wisdom figure, a human artisan endowed with the same creative wisdom by which God fashioned the world. The LXX renders this as pneuma theion sophias kai suneseōs kai epistēmēs ("a divine spirit of wisdom and understanding and knowledge"), where pneuma theion ("divine spirit") corresponds to ruach Elohim. The term melakhah ("workmanship") in v. 3 is the same word used for the "work" of creation in Genesis 2:2-3, further tightening the creation-Tabernacle parallel. Notably, this is the first instance in the Torah where an individual is said to be "filled with the Spirit of God" -- a designation that anticipates the prophetic endowment (Num 11:25; 1 Sam 10:6) and ultimately the messianic anointing (Isa 11:2; 61:1).

**Oholiav ben Achisamakh** (v. 6) -- "Oholiab son of Ahisamach." The name Oholiav means "the Father is my tent" or "tent of the Father" (from 'ohel, "tent" + 'av, "father"), another name whose meaning directly corresponds to the artisan's vocation: the one whose name proclaims "the Father is my tent" is appointed to build the tent of the divine Father. The pairing of Bezalel from Judah with Oholiab from Dan is theologically significant. Judah is the preeminent tribe, the first-born of Leah's sons in terms of leadership and destiny; Dan is the son of Bilhah, Rachel's maidservant, among the lowest-ranking tribes by birth. Their collaboration in building the Tabernacle demonstrates that the sacred work requires the contribution of the entire community, not merely its elite. The LXX renders the name as Eliab huion Amisadak ek tēs phulēs Dan ("Eliab son of Amisadak of the tribe of Dan"). The Vaticanus reading "Eliab" rather than "Oholiab" represents a consistent LXX variant for this name, apparently reflecting a different Hebrew Vorlage or an early scribal substitution.

**shabbtotay** (v. 13) -- "My Sabbaths." The possessive suffix (-ay, "My") attached to shabbatot (plural of shabbat) emphasizes YHWH's ownership of the Sabbath institution. The Sabbaths are not merely Israel's days of rest but YHWH's own appointed times, entrusted to Israel as a covenantal sign. The plural form (shabbtotay rather than shabbatti) may encompass not only the weekly Sabbath but also the sabbatical year (Lev 25:2-7) and the Jubilee (Lev 25:8-12), all of which participate in the Sabbath principle. The placement of this Sabbath instruction immediately after the Tabernacle construction commands (chs. 25-31) is deliberately juxtaposed: the work of building YHWH's dwelling must not override the observance of YHWH's rest. Even the most sacred labor -- the construction of the very house of God -- does not supersede the Sabbath commandment. The rabbinical tradition (b. Shabbat 49b, 73a) derives the thirty-nine categories of prohibited Sabbath labor (melakhot) from the types of work required for Tabernacle construction, making this juxtaposition the legal basis of Sabbath halakhah. The LXX renders this as ta sabbata mou ("my sabbaths").

**ot** (vv. 13, 17) -- "sign." The noun 'ot designates a visible marker or indicator of a deeper reality. The Sabbath is designated an 'ot ("sign") between YHWH and Israel in the same way that the rainbow is an 'ot of the Noahic covenant (Gen 9:12-17) and circumcision is an 'ot of the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 17:11). Each of the three great covenants thus has its own sign: rainbow (creation order), circumcision (genealogical identity), Sabbath (sanctification and sacred time). The Sabbath sign is unique in that it is temporal rather than physical -- it is inscribed in time rather than on the body or in the sky. This makes it, in one sense, the most demanding sign, since it must be actively and repeatedly observed rather than passively displayed. The purpose clause lada'at ki ani YHWH meqaddishkhem ("to know that I am YHWH who sanctifies you," v. 13) connects the Sabbath sign to divine sanctification: the observance of the Sabbath both demonstrates and effects Israel's consecration by YHWH. The LXX renders 'ot as sēmeion ("sign, signal"), the same term used in the Fourth Gospel for the miraculous signs of Jesus (John 2:11; 4:54; etc.).

**berit olam** (v. 16) -- "a covenant forever" or "an everlasting covenant." The phrase berit 'olam designates the Sabbath as a permanent covenantal institution binding YHWH and Israel. The same phrase is applied to the Noahic covenant (Gen 9:16), the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 17:7, 13, 19), the priestly covenant of salt (Num 18:19), the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 23:5), and the new covenant (Isa 55:3; Jer 32:40; Ezek 37:26). The Sabbath's designation as berit 'olam places it in the most exalted category of divine commitment. The LXX renders this as diathēkēn aiōnion ("an everlasting covenant"), using the term diathēkē that the New Testament adopts for the "new covenant" (Luke 22:20; 1 Cor 11:25; Heb 8:8-13).

**shney luchot ha'edut, luchot even, ketuvim be'etsba Elohim** (v. 18) -- "the two tablets of the Testimony, tablets of stone, written by the finger of God." This verse introduces three descriptions of the tablets in ascending theological significance: (1) luchot ha'edut -- "tablets of the Testimony," identifying their content as the 'edut, the stipulations of the covenant that also give the Ark its name (aron ha'edut); (2) luchot even -- "tablets of stone," identifying their material as the most permanent medium of ancient inscription; (3) ketuvim be'etsba Elohim -- "written by the finger of God," identifying their author as God Himself. The phrase etsba Elohim ("finger of God") appears elsewhere in Exodus 8:15 [Heb 8:19], where the Egyptian magicians recognize YHWH's plague as "the finger of God," and in Deuteronomy 9:10, which repeats this verse's testimony. The "finger of God" signifies direct, personal divine action without intermediary. Unlike the prophetic word, which comes through human speech, and unlike the law as later written by Moses (Deut 31:9, 24), these tablets bear YHWH's own inscription -- they are, in the most literal sense, divine autograph. The LXX renders this as duo plakas tou marturiou, plakas lithinas gegrammenas tō daktulō tou theou ("two tablets of the testimony, stone tablets written by the finger of God"). The term marturion ("testimony, witness") is the LXX's consistent translation of 'edut, and it carries forward into the New Testament as the term for apostolic witness (Acts 4:33; 1 Cor 1:6).

### Grammatical Notes

**va'amalle oto ruach Elohim** (v. 3) -- "And I have filled him with the Spirit of God." The verb male' (Piel: "to fill") takes a double accusative construction: the person filled (oto, "him") and the substance with which he is filled (ruach Elohim, "the Spirit of God"). The Piel stem intensifies the action, indicating a thorough and complete filling. This is divine passive made active -- it is YHWH Himself who fills (first person: va'amalle, "I have filled"), not an impersonal spiritual force. The four nouns that follow (chokhmah, tevunah, da'at, melakhah) are introduced by the preposition be- ("in, with"), specifying the domains in which the Spirit's filling manifests. The construction implies that wisdom, understanding, knowledge, and workmanship are not natural aptitudes enhanced by the Spirit, but rather gifts constituted by the Spirit's presence -- Bezalel possesses these abilities because and insofar as the Spirit fills him.

**akh et-shabbtotay tishmoru** (v. 13) -- "Surely My Sabbaths you shall keep." The particle 'akh functions as a restrictive or emphatic particle. In this context it carries both force: emphatically, "surely, indeed you shall keep My Sabbaths"; restrictively, "only/above all My Sabbaths you shall keep," implying that the Sabbath takes precedence even over the Tabernacle work just commanded. The fronting of et-shabbtotay before the verb tishmoru creates emphatic word order (Object-Verb rather than the default Verb-Object), placing maximum stress on the Sabbath as the topic. The verb shamar ("to keep, guard, observe") is the same verb used for Adam's charge to "keep" the garden (Gen 2:15, lesham'rah), reinforcing the Eden-Tabernacle typology: Israel's Sabbath-keeping corresponds to Adam's garden-keeping.

**mot yumat** (v. 14) -- "he shall surely be put to death." The infinitive absolute (mot) preceding the finite verb (yumat, Hophal imperfect) is the standard Hebrew construction for the death penalty formula, expressing absolute certainty of the punishment. The Hophal stem (yumat, "shall be caused to die") indicates judicial execution -- not natural death or divine punishment, but execution carried out by the community. The combination of mot yumat with venikhretah ("shall be cut off") in the same verse has generated extensive discussion: if the profaner is executed, the additional penalty of "cutting off" seems redundant. The rabbinic tradition distinguishes the two: mot yumat applies when there are witnesses and a court to impose the sentence, while karet (cutting off) is a divine punishment that applies when the violation occurs without witnesses. The LXX renders mot yumat as thanatō thanatoustai ("by death he shall be put to death"), reproducing the Hebrew infinitive-absolute construction with the Greek cognate dative.

**shavat vayyinnafash** (v. 17) -- "He ceased and was refreshed." The two verbs describe YHWH's Sabbath rest in Genesis 2:2-3, here cited as the theological ground of Israel's Sabbath observance. The verb shavat (from sh-b-t, the root of shabbat) means "to cease, to stop, to rest from labor." The verb naphash (from n-p-sh, the root of nefesh, "soul, life, breath") means "to be refreshed, to catch one's breath, to take a breather." The anthropomorphism is striking and deliberate: YHWH is said to have "caught His breath" after the work of creation, as a laborer rests after exertion. This is not a statement about divine fatigue (cf. Isa 40:28, "YHWH does not grow weary") but a revelatory accommodation: YHWH describes His own rest in terms that Israel can emulate. The nifal form vayyinnafash ("and He was refreshed") suggests a reflexive action -- YHWH refreshed Himself, delighted in the completion of His work. The LXX renders shavat vayyinnafash as katepausen kai epausato ("He rested and ceased"), losing the distinctive naphash ("refreshed") in favor of a second verb of cessation.

**kekhaloto ledabber itto** (v. 18) -- "when He finished speaking with him." The infinitive construct kekhalloto (from k-l-h, "to finish, complete," in Piel) with the preposition ke- ("when, as") creates a temporal clause: "when He had completed." The object of the completion is ledabber ("to speak, speaking"), and the prepositional phrase itto ("with him") indicates mutual communication -- not merely speaking to Moses but speaking with him. The same verb (k-l-h, Piel) is used in Genesis 2:1-2 for the completion of creation: vaykhal Elohim... melakhto ("and God finished... His work"). The parallel is architecturally precise: God completes His speaking about the Tabernacle (kekhaloto ledabber) just as God completed His work of creation (vayekhal... melakhto), and the result in both cases is a sacred space -- the cosmos and the Tabernacle -- followed by rest.

### Structural Notes

Exodus 31 serves as the conclusion to the entire Tabernacle instruction sequence that began in Exodus 25:1. The chapter divides into three sections of decreasing length but increasing theological weight: the appointment of the artisans (vv. 1-11), the Sabbath commandment (vv. 12-17), and the giving of the tablets (v. 18). This tripartite structure moves from the practical (who will build), to the temporal (when they must stop building), to the transcendent (the divine inscription that grounds the entire enterprise).

The appointment of Bezalel and Oholiab (vv. 1-11) answers a question implicit in the preceding six chapters of detailed Tabernacle instructions: who possesses the skill to execute these designs? The answer is that no one possesses it naturally -- it must be given by the Spirit of God. The catalogue of Tabernacle items in vv. 7-11 recapitulates the entire instruction sequence of chapters 25-30 in compressed form, functioning as a summary checklist: the Tent of Meeting, the Ark, the mercy seat, the table, the lampstand, the incense altar, the burnt offering altar, the basin, the priestly garments, the anointing oil, and the fragrant incense. The phrase kekhol asher tsivvitikha ya'asu ("according to all that I have commanded you they shall do," v. 11) forms an inclusio with the opening instruction of 25:9, binding the entire Tabernacle corpus into a unified divine command.

The Sabbath passage (vv. 12-17) is strategically placed between the Tabernacle instructions and the golden calf narrative. Its position establishes a critical theological priority: the sanctification of time takes precedence over the construction of sacred space. The Sabbath existed before the Tabernacle (Gen 2:2-3; Exod 16:23-30; 20:8-11) and will outlast it. The designation of the Sabbath as both 'ot ("sign," vv. 13, 17) and berit 'olam ("everlasting covenant," v. 16) elevates it above any particular structure or institution. The double death penalty (mot yumat and karet in v. 14) underscores the gravity of Sabbath violation in terms that exceed even the penalties for misuse of the sacred anointing oil or incense (30:33, 38, where only karet is specified). The Sabbath is thus presented as more sacred than the Tabernacle itself -- a remarkable theological claim given the extraordinary care lavished on the Tabernacle's design.

Verse 18 stands as one of the most theologically dense single verses in the Torah. It serves simultaneously as the conclusion of the Sinai revelation (chs. 19-31), the transition to the golden calf crisis (ch. 32), and the introduction of the tablets that will be shattered and then renewed (chs. 32-34). The verse's temporal marker kekhaloto ("when He finished") echoes the language of creation's completion, while the phrase be'etsba Elohim ("by the finger of God") establishes the tablets as the only objects in the biblical narrative directly inscribed by God's own hand. The narrative tension is devastating: at the very moment YHWH finishes speaking with Moses and entrusts to him the divine autograph of the covenant, the people below are constructing the golden calf (32:1-6). The tablets of the Testimony, still warm from the finger of God, are about to be shattered by the hands of Moses.

### Theological Notes

The appointment of Bezalel introduces a theology of Spirit-empowered craftsmanship that has no parallel in ancient Near Eastern religious literature. In surrounding cultures, temples are built by royal decree and corvee labor; in Israel, the dwelling of God is built by Spirit-filled artisans working with the voluntary offerings of the people (cf. 25:2; 35:21-29). The fourfold endowment of chokhmah, tevunah, da'at, and melakhah (v. 3) corresponds to the fourfold description of YHWH's creative wisdom in Proverbs 3:19-20: "YHWH by wisdom (chokhmah) founded the earth; by understanding (tevunah) He established the heavens; by His knowledge (da'at) the depths were broken up." The Tabernacle artisan works with the same wisdom by which God created the world, because the Tabernacle is a microcosm -- a miniature replica of the cosmic temple. The creation-Tabernacle parallel is further reinforced by the seven divine speeches of chapters 25-31 (25:1; 30:11; 30:17; 30:22; 30:34; 31:1; 31:12), which correspond to the seven days of creation in Genesis 1, with the Sabbath command of 31:12-17 corresponding to the seventh day of rest.

The Sabbath theology of vv. 12-17 weaves together three dimensions of the Sabbath: (1) the creational dimension -- the Sabbath is grounded in YHWH's rest after creation (v. 17, echoing Gen 2:2-3 and the fourth commandment of Exod 20:8-11); (2) the covenantal dimension -- the Sabbath is a "sign" ('ot) and an "everlasting covenant" (berit 'olam), a mutual commitment between YHWH and Israel (vv. 13, 16-17); and (3) the sanctifying dimension -- the Sabbath reveals that YHWH is "the one who sanctifies you" (meqaddishkhem, v. 13). The Sabbath is thus not merely a day of rest but a sacramental sign that effects what it signifies: by keeping the Sabbath, Israel participates in God's own rest and is thereby sanctified. The death penalty for Sabbath violation (vv. 14-15) is not arbitrary severity but reflects the Sabbath's covenantal status: to profane the Sabbath is to repudiate the covenant, and the penalty for covenant repudiation is covenant exclusion (karet) and death (mot yumat).

The verb vayyinnafash ("and He was refreshed," v. 17) is one of the most daring anthropomorphisms in the Torah. The root n-p-sh connects the divine rest to the concept of nefesh ("soul, life, breath"): God "ensouled Himself," "re-breathed," "took delight" in the completed creation. This verb appears only three times in the Hebrew Bible (Exod 23:12; 31:17; 2 Sam 16:14), and in the other two occurrences it refers to human refreshment -- a slave resting on the Sabbath (Exod 23:12) and David refreshing himself after Absalom's rebellion (2 Sam 16:14). The application of this deeply human verb to YHWH declares that the Sabbath is not merely a cessation but a positive delight, an enjoyment of completed work, a divine exhalation of satisfaction. Israel's Sabbath rest is an imitation of and participation in this divine refreshment.

The giving of the tablets (v. 18) represents the climactic moment of the Sinai theophany: the divine word becomes tangible, material, permanent. The shift from spoken word (ledabber, "speaking") to written word (ketuvim, "written") marks a transition from the ephemeral to the enduring, from voice to document, from theophany to testimony. The tablets are called luchot ha'edut ("tablets of the Testimony"), and the noun 'edut ("testimony, witness") characterizes the covenant not as a contract between equals but as a witnessed declaration of the sovereign's will. The 'edut testifies to what YHWH has said and what Israel has committed to obey. The material -- stone -- is the most permanent medium available, as if YHWH intended these words to outlast all other human inscriptions. And the mode of writing -- be'etsba Elohim ("by the finger of God") -- elevates the tablets beyond any prophetic or scribal text. Moses himself will later write torah on scrolls (Deut 31:9, 24), but these tablets bear no human authorship. They are, uniquely in the biblical canon, the direct handwriting of God. The devastating irony of the canonical narrative is that these uniquely divine artifacts will be the first thing destroyed in the golden calf crisis (32:19) -- and then, in an act of breathtaking grace, rewritten (34:1, 28).

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 31 appears on pages 74-75, with the chapter beginning in the right column of page 74, marked with the section number KE (= 25) in the LXX section numbering. At v. 2, the LXX renders the calling of Bezalel as idou anakeklēka tō onomati ton Beseleel ton tou Ouriou ton Ōr ek phulēs Iouda ("Behold, I have called by name Beseleël, the son of Ourios, the son of Ōr, of the tribe of Judah"), using the perfect tense anakeklēka ("I have called out/summoned") for the Hebrew qarati ("I have called"). At v. 3, the Spirit's endowment is rendered kai eneplēsa auton pneuma theion sophias kai suneseōs kai epistēmēs ("and I have filled him with a divine spirit of wisdom and understanding and knowledge"), where pneuma theion ("divine spirit") renders the MT's ruach Elohim. The LXX's use of theion ("divine") rather than theou ("of God") may represent a slight theological adjustment, emphasizing the quality of the spirit rather than its personal source. At v. 6, the Vaticanus reads Eliab rather than Oholiab (MT 'Oholiav), a variant that persists throughout the LXX tradition for this name. At v. 10, the Vaticanus includes tas stolas tas leitourgikass ("the liturgical robes"), rendering the MT's bigdey hasserad ("woven garments/garments of service") with a specifically liturgical Greek term. At v. 13, the Sabbath command renders 'ot as sēmeion ("sign") and the knowledge clause as eidenai hoti egō kurios ho hagiazōn humas ("to know that I am the Lord who sanctifies you"), where kurios ("Lord") renders YHWH according to the standard LXX convention. At v. 15, the LXX renders shabbat shabbaton qodesh laYHWH as sabbata anapausis hagia tō kuriō ("a sabbath rest holy to the Lord"), where anapausis ("rest, cessation") translates the superlative shabbaton. At v. 17, the critical verb vayyinnafash is rendered kai katepausen ("and He rested/ceased"), which flattens the MT's anthropomorphic "was refreshed" into the more theologically neutral "ceased" -- an apparent theological smoothing characteristic of the LXX translators. At v. 18, the tablets are rendered as duo plakas tou marturiou lithinas gegrammenas tō daktulō tou theou ("two stone tablets of the testimony written by the finger of God"), closely corresponding to the MT. The term marturion ("testimony") for 'edut is the standard LXX rendering that enters the New Testament vocabulary as the basis for the apostolic concept of "witness" and "testimony" (marturion/marturia).

--- LOGOS
